questionText: What is the difference between abstract class and interface?
answerText: Abstract class: can have both abstract and concrete methods, state, constructors. Interface: only abstract methods (until Java 8), no state, no constructors. A class can implement multiple interfaces but extend only one abstract class. Use interfaces for contracts, abstract classes for shared implementation.

Java automatically manages memory through garbage collection. When objects are no longer reachable, the GC reclaims their memory. Algorithms include mark-and-sweep, copying, generational collection. Developers shouldn't rely on explicit garbage collection (System.gc() is just a suggestion).

questionText: What are Java generics and why are they useful?
answerText: Generics provide compile-time type safety for collections and other data structures. Enable writing generic algorithms that work with different types. Reduce need for casting. Syntax: List<String> list = new ArrayList<>(). Type erasure removes generic information at runtime.
Use eager initialization, static block, or double-checked locking. Best approach: use enum (most thread-safe) or static inner class. Avoid synchronized methods for performance.

questionText: What is the purpose of the volatile keyword?
answerText: volatile ensures that reads and writes to a variable are atomic and visible across threads. Prevents compiler optimizations that might cache the value. Doesn't provide mutual exclusion like synchronized. Used for flags and status variables.
